title: Greenfield Rewrite Planning
use_for: planning a new implementation that preserves product intent but intentionally changes architecture
separating requirement extraction from implementation copying
turning an existing codebase into a source of domain knowledge and anti-patterns
defining rewrite phases before code is moved or rebuilt
do_not_use_for: bug fixes where the existing architecture remains authoritative
rewrites without a validation or requirement extraction plan
rules: treat the old implementation as evidence, not as the target architecture
preserve product intent explicitly before replacing structure
separate domain requirements, runtime architecture, infrastructure boundaries, and migration mechanics
define what the new system must prove before implementation begins
avoid copying old shapes unless they survive a design review
procedure: State why a new design is required and which old constraints are no longer accepted.
Inventory the old system by product capability, domain concept, integration point, state owner, and failure mode.
Classify old behavior as preserve intent, redesign, delete, investigate, or do not copy.
Choose the new architectural frame: runtime host, modules, boundaries, pipelines, diagnostics, and build separation.
Define implementation slices around domain outcomes rather than old folders.
Define validation artifacts for each slice: tests, probes, snapshots, logs, diagnostics, or manual smoke paths.
Record unresolved legacy questions without blocking unrelated new design work.

primary_when: the task intentionally replaces the old architecture rather than preserving it
the user wants to rebuild a project around a new foundation while retaining selected product intent
must_not_own_when: the request requires behavior-preserving structural cleanup
the old architecture is still the accepted design authority

solves: how to begin a rewrite without blindly copying the old system
how to keep product intent while rejecting old architectural form
how to define slices, validation, and decision records for a new foundation

core_principles: A rewrite is not a large refactor; behavior may be intentionally redesigned.
Legacy code can be a requirement source and a warning source at the same time.
The new architecture must be judged against explicit domain needs, not old folder names.
Validation must be planned before the old implementation stops compiling.
constraints: Do not encode project names, local paths, product identities, or one-off class names into the plan.
Do not treat the old implementation as canonical architecture.
Do not delete old code when it still carries unresolved requirements; archive it outside the build instead.
Do not start broad implementation before capability boundaries and validation slices are named.
decision_rules: If old code shows a useful behavior with poor structure, preserve the intent and redesign the shape.
If old code only exists to compensate for a bad foundation, mark it as do not copy until proven otherwise.
If a capability cannot be validated, split it smaller or add observability before implementing it.
If a dependency is infrastructure-specific, isolate it before modeling domain mechanics.
If the rewrite plan starts mirroring old folders, reframe around domain capabilities and runtime ownership.

preferred_patterns: explicit runtime host and module ownership
diagnostics and validation built into early slices
avoid_patterns: renaming a legacy architecture and calling it new
copying static global state into a new foundation
using old folders as the rewrite roadmap
postponing diagnostics until after feature parity
deleting legacy evidence before requirements are extracted

recipes.name: Create a rewrite map
recipes.when: Use before implementation starts for a system whose old architecture is intentionally being replaced.
steps: List product capabilities in user-facing or domain-facing language.
Map each capability to old evidence, domain concepts, integrations, state, and side effects.
Classify old evidence as preserve intent, redesign, delete, investigate, or do not copy.
Define the new runtime and boundary model that will own the capability.
Define the minimum validation required before the slice is considered reconstructed.

pitfalls: Calling a rewrite a refactor and accidentally preserving the wrong architecture.
Losing edge cases because legacy code was moved or ignored without classification.
Designing infrastructure first and forcing domain mechanics to fit it.
Rebuilding hidden global state under cleaner names.
review_checklist: Is the task clearly a rewrite rather than a behavior-preserving refactor?
Is legacy code archived and classified instead of deleted?
Are domain capabilities separated from infrastructure mechanisms?
Are implementation slices independent enough to validate?
Are project-specific names and paths absent from reusable guidance?

references.title: Refactoring
references.interpretation: Use only to distinguish behavior-preserving refactoring from intentional rewrite planning.
references.why: It prevents confusing a rewrite with an ordinary refactor.

references.title: Contracts
references.interpretation: Use when rewrite slices need explicit preserved or redesigned contracts.
references.why: Rewrites still need visible contracts and validation boundaries.
